artifacts.content:
Sofi,

I’m hiding in the truck for ten minutes before the lunch rush and figured I’d actually write instead of just sending you memes.

I keep thinking about how much of our menu is basically a medical file now. Since Mateo’s celiac diagnosis last March, I’ve been so paranoid. That $900 dedicated fryer I bought in April 2024 just for his gluten‑free tortilla chips and churro bites is still the best/worst impulse purchase I’ve ever made. At least now he can eat the gluten‑free kids’ cheese quesadilla from the truck without me panicking about cross‑contamination.

And then there’s Mamá. I’m still proud every time someone orders “Carmen’s Heart Helper” and has no idea it came from her mild heart attack at Lions Gate in June 2023 and that low‑sugar hibiscus‑lime recipe her cardiologist gave us. She gets this little smile when she sees the name on the board.

Now Papá with the Type 2 diabetes since September 2024, and I’m here testing “diabetic‑friendly churro bites” at midnight at West Van Shared Kitchen, trying to get the sugar down to around 3 g a serving so he can still have something sweet. Half the time I’m doing that from 10:30 p.m. to 2:00 a.m. because we fell two months behind on commissary fees after his heart attack and the $3,000 we pulled from the business to fly Mamá up.

Some days it feels like the truck is just one big love letter to our family’s health problems. But then a hiker bites into a taco and says it’s the best thing they’ve eaten all week, and it kind of feels worth it.

Love,
D

metadata.subject: Our family is basically my menu now

artifacts.content:
Hey Elena,

I’m at the commissary on Marine again, waiting for the rice to finish, and just wanted to get my head straight about all this health stuff.

Ever since my borderline A1C at West Vancouver Community Health Centre back in May 2024, I can’t look at a Jarritos crate the same way. I’ve already told the supplier I’m capping orders at two crates a week and I’m going to push the sparkling water and the hibiscus agua fresca harder. Between that and Mamá’s “Heart Helper” drink and Marco’s “Sober Choice” agua fresca we started for his 33rd birthday last July after he’d been in the Lions Gate outpatient program since early 2023, the board is starting to look like a wellness retreat.

Add in Papá’s diabetes churro bites and Mateo’s gluten‑free fryer, and it’s like every family diagnosis ends up on my prep list. I was re‑writing the dessert section last night at 12:30 a.m. at West Van Shared Kitchen, trying to keep the “diabetic‑friendly churro bites” around 3 g sugar so Papá can eat them, while Mateo was half‑asleep doing homework in the little office area.

I know I joke about it, but it honestly helps me make better choices too. If I’m telling customers to pick the unsweetened sparkling water, I can’t be pounding three sodas a night.

Anyway, I’ll be home as soon as I finish cooling the carnitas (properly on the new rack, don’t worry). Save me some leftovers?

Te quiero,
D

metadata.subject: Menu is starting to look like a medical chart
metadata.location: West Van Shared Kitchen, 1450 Marine Dr

artifacts.content:
Ops / sanity check – late 2025

1. Commissary schedule
- Still stuck with the 10:30 p.m.–2:00 a.m. slot at West Van Shared Kitchen because of being two months behind on fees after Papá’s heart attack at Lions Gate in October 2024 and the $3,000 we pulled from the Howe Sound Taco Co. account for their travel.
- Remember: keep all marinated meats, cooked beans, and salsas on the locked rolling rack in the shared walk‑in, clearly labeled “Daniel Morales – Howe Sound Taco Co.” so nobody “borrows” anything.

2. Safety / health inspections
- May 3, 2023: Karen Lee (VCH) written warning at 1450 Marine for leaving hot carnitas in the refrigerated van during my panic attack. Since then: added extra stainless prep table + dedicated cooling rack; absolutely no cooling in the van.
- Feb 12, 2024: small grease flare‑up at West Van Shared Kitchen frying churros; singed right forearm hair, signed incident report with building manager. All deep‑frying now ONLY on truck under hood – commissary = beans, rice, baking.

3. Kids and commissary
- Since Sept 2024: Tues/Thu 5:30–7:30 a.m. “Commissary prep + Mateo homework” block. He stays in office area only – no entry to prep area per VCH guidelines.

4. Home kitchen rules
- After March 2024 complaint about fryer smells triggering downstairs neighbor’s asthma at #203 – 1450 Bellevue, absolutely no commercial cooking at home. Only dry tasks: portion spices, fold wrappers.

5. Mental note
- Anxiety spikes = slow down, don’t improvise. Last time it cost me a warning and a month of stomach knots.

Mamá,

I just dropped off a batch of “Carmen’s Heart Helper” at the truck and thought of you, so I’m writing before the line starts.

Do you remember those first weeks after your mild heart attack at Lions Gate in June 2023, when we were sitting in that tiny cardiology office and the doctor was explaining how to keep the sugar around 6–8 g per 16 oz? I was so scared, but also already thinking, “How do I turn this into something you’ll actually enjoy?” Now people order your hibiscus‑lime agua fresca all the time and don’t know it came from that moment.

Papá’s doing the same to me now with his Type 2 diabetes from September 2024. I’ve been testing those low‑sugar churro bites since then, trying to keep them around 3 g per serving. I told him I’m going to list them as a “diabetic‑friendly churro bite” on the truck so he doesn’t feel left out when everyone else orders dessert.

And of course, Mateo with his celiac since March 2024. That’s why I added the gluten‑free kids’ cheese quesadilla and the dedicated fryer on the truck last April – so he can eat safely when he visits and we don’t worry about crumbs.

Sometimes I feel like I spend more time reading labels than cooking. But when I see you at the window, ordering your drink with that little proud smile, it reminds me why I’m doing all this.

Tell Papá I’m bringing him a test batch of the new churros on Sunday.

Con mucho cariño,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Your drink, Papá’s churros, and Mateo’s quesadillas

artifacts.content:
Event: Health + permits admin catch‑up
Time: Saturday, 2025-11-08, 8:00–11:00 a.m.
Location: Dining table, #203 – 1450 Bellevue Ave
Attendees: Daniel (maybe Elena if kids are occupied)

Agenda / notes:

1. Vancouver Coastal Health follow‑up
- Re‑read notes from April 4, 2025 inspection at Howe Sound Community Centre with Karen Liu.
- Finish drafting written outbreak‑response plan tied to August 2023 norovirus incident at West Vancouver Night Market so I can sleep at night knowing we’re ready for next season.

2. Fire + safety
- Email West Vancouver Fire & Rescue (16th Street station) to ask if Captain James O’Reilly’s conditional approval from Oct 18, 2024 (after the small grease fire by Park Royal and the temporary propane line repair) needs upgrading to a full clearance.
- Double‑check that all deep‑frying is documented as “truck only” after the Feb 12, 2024 grease flare‑up at West Van Shared Kitchen.

3. Money / compliance
- Follow up with BC Ministry of Finance auditor Lisa McKinnon about the June 12, 2024 PST audit and those 3 months of unreported late‑2022 revenue; confirm if any updated tax‑compliance letter is still pending.
- Check status of WorkSafeBC clearance letter related to Miguel’s July 2023 burn hearing at the Victoria Drive office.

4. Bylaw / noise
- Ping Andrea Patel (Howe & Finch LLP) for update on formal resolution letter from District of North Vancouver re: April 9, 2024 parking + noise complaint hearing.

5. Reward
- If I finish all this before 11, take kids for hot chocolate and a Spirit Trail walk.

Hey Jay,

Catching up between orders at the office park spot.

You asked how I’m handling all the “good neighbour” stuff. Honestly, it’s a mix of guilt and spreadsheets. After my PCR came back positive on Dec 14, 2021 and Dr. Priya Nair at West Van Medical basically told me I probably caught COVID during that insane Friday service, I’ve been strict: box of rapid tests and KN95s lives in a labeled bin under the prep table, and anyone with symptoms tests before stepping in the truck.

Then January 2022 hit and my whole family got COVID. Mamá ended up on oxygen for three days at Lions Gate. Watching her in that bed is why I still keep spacing in the line and ask my sister Sofia to only bring Mateo and Lucia by during slower weekday afternoons, so they’re not in the middle of a crush of people during respiratory season.

On the noise side, I dragged Papá behind our building at 1450 Bellevue in August 2023 to test the new inverter generator. With his noise‑induced hearing loss from all those North Van construction years, he’s my decibel meter. We used an app and made sure it stayed under 60 dB at 10 metres. No speakers, no music – just the sizzle of the griddle.

It all costs time and money (don’t get me started on the extra $280/month for compostables since May 2022, right after I finished paying the $3,200 GST arrears), but it feels better than risking another outbreak or pissing off neighbours.

How are you handling masks these days?

– Daniel

metadata.subject: Noise, COVID, and trying not to be THAT truck

artifacts.content:
Family + truck balance – reminders for myself

1. Mateo’s asthma + truck schedule
- Non‑negotiable: fryers OFF by 7:30 p.m. on school nights if the truck is parking anywhere near #203 – 1450 Bellevue. After his ER visit for an asthma flare on Oct 18, 2023 and talking to Dr. Aaron McLeod at BC Children’s, I’m not gambling with lingering oil smoke again.
- No late‑night heavy prep runs past his bedtime when the truck is close to the building.

2. Kids around crowds
- Ask Sofia to keep bringing Mateo and Lucia only during slow weekday afternoons, especially in fall/winter. After Mom’s three days on oxygen at Lions Gate during our Jan 2022 COVID mess, I can’t relax when they’re in the middle of a packed lineup.

3. Noise + neighbours
- Stick with the quiet inverter generator Papá and I tested behind the building in Aug 2023 – decibel app reading under 60 dB at 10 m. No amplified music, ever.

4. Packaging + money stress
- Remember the conversation with Marco on Granville: we chose the extra ~$280/month for fully compostable packaging in May 2022 right after finishing the $3,200 CRA GST payment plan because it’s cheaper than another shutdown.

5. Mental health
- If anxiety starts creeping toward panic (like the May 3, 2023 carnitas incident in the van), step away, breathe, call Elena or Sofi. Do NOT improvise with food safety or equipment.

Goal: Protect the kids, respect the neighbours, keep the truck running without burning myself out.

Notes to self:

- Applicant Phone: (604) 555-2378
- Dates Requested: 2026-05-15 to 2026-09-30 (Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays, 11:00–19:00)
- Completed Agreement Form With Certificate Of Insurance Naming The Village Of Lions Bay As Additional Insured Attached: true
- Photos Of The Food Vending Unit Showing All Sides Attached: true
- Dimensions Of Food Vending Unit Length: 20 ft (truck body length, excluding hitch)
- Dimensions Of Food Vending Unit Width: 8 ft (standard width, excluding mirrors)
- Dimensions Of Food Vending Unit Height: 11 ft (ground to top of rooftop ventilation hood)
- Number Of Employees: 4.0
- Health Authority Approval Attached: true
